programs.name: Medical Assistance (MA) for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Minnesota’s Medical Assistance program, the state’s Medicaid, helps low-income seniors gain access to healthcare services. Members choose a managed care plan based on their county and ability to pay, or receive fee-for-service if not enrolled.[4][6]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and people with disabilities aged 65+ who meet income and asset limits.
programs.benefit_value: Varies by plan and services; covers health care, home care, nursing home costs, and Medicare premiums for eligible.

programs.name: Elderly Waiver (EW) and Alternative Care (AC) Program
programs.what_it_does: These programs fund home and community-based services (HCBS) for people 65+ who require nursing home level care but choose community living. They provide services to help seniors stay in their homes and delay or prevent nursing facility placement.[1]
programs.who_its_for: People 65+ needing nursing home level of care, meeting financial eligibility, preferring home/community settings.
programs.benefit_value: Services within case mix budget caps (e.g., monthly rate limits); rates per Long-Term Services and Supports Rate Limits (DHS-3945), such as $141 daily for some periods in 2025-2026.

programs.name: Minnesota Senior Health Options (MSHO)
programs.what_it_does: MSHO combines Medical Assistance (Medicaid) and Medicare into one health plan package for seniors. A care coordinator assists with health care and support services.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Integrated coverage of Medicare and Medicaid benefits including long-term services; no additional premiums for most.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) including QMB, SLMB, QI
programs.what_it_does: These programs help pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments for low-income seniors. Assistance includes applications via Senior LinkAge Line for QMB (full coverage), SLMB, and QI.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income Medicare beneficiaries 65+ meeting specific income/asset thresholds (e.g., QMB up to 100% FPL).
programs.benefit_value: QMB: Full Part A/B premiums/costs; SLMB: Part B premium (~$185/mo in 2026); QI: Partial Part B help.

programs.name: Minnesota SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program) / Food Support
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly benefits via EBT card to buy food for low-income households. Seniors can get applications and help through Senior LinkAge Line.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ and households meeting income tests; higher asset limits for elderly/disabled.
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per person; max benefits scale by household size/income (e.g., up to $291/single in 2026).

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP) / Energy Assistance
programs.what_it_does: Provides grants to help with heating costs and crisis assistance. Senior LinkAge Line assists with applications.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors 60+ at risk of energy crisis; income up to 50% state median.
programs.benefit_value: Heating grants $150-$1,000+; crisis benefits up to $1,500 depending on need/fuel type.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: Funds energy efficiency upgrades like insulation and furnace repairs for low-income homes. Reduces energy costs long-term for seniors.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors 60+; priority for elderly, disabled, families with children.
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements valued $5,000-$15,000 per household; lifetime energy savings.

programs.name: Senior Health Insurance Program (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers free counseling on Medicare, supplements, and prescription help. Part of Senior LinkAge Line services statewide.[5]
programs.who_its_for: All Minnesota Medicare beneficiaries 65+, caregivers, and family.
programs.benefit_value: Free personalized advising; helps save thousands on premiums/drugs annually.

programs.name: Senior Nutrition Program / Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors and provides congregate dining. Promotes health and reduces food insecurity.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound or congregate-site seniors 60+ who are frail, disabled, or low-income.

programs.name: Family Adult Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite, counseling, and support services to family caregivers of seniors. Helps caregivers manage stress and continue providing care.
programs.who_its_for: Unpaid family caregivers of Minnesotans 60+ or 18+ with Alzheimer's.
programs.benefit_value: Respite grants up to $2,000/year; counseling and training at no/low cost.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time paid training and community service jobs to unemployed low-income seniors. Builds skills for unsubsidized employment.
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income 55+ unable to find work; priority for hardest-to-serve.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage work ~20 hrs/week; leads to regular jobs for many.

programs.name: Legal Assistance for Seniors (LAS)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal help for income-qualified seniors on issues like housing, benefits, abuse. Operates through Legal Aid Society and partners.
programs.benefit_value: Free advice/representation; resolves issues worth thousands in benefits/housing.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in nursing homes, assisted living, and board/LODGING facilities. Investigates complaints and ensures quality care.
programs.who_its_for: Residents 18+ in long-term care facilities and their families.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy; resolves 80%+ complaints without escalation.

programs.name: Minnesota Senior Care Plus
programs.what_it_does: This managed care program covers health care and Elderly Waiver services for eligible seniors. It integrates Medical Assistance benefits for those not dually eligible with Medicare.[3]
programs.who_its_for: People aged 65 or older with Medical Assistance (with or without Medicare); automatic enrollment if no choice made upon turning 65.[3]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific managed care plan combining Medicaid with Elderly Waiver services, tailored for Minnesota seniors transitioning from younger adult programs.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Full coverage of health care and waiver services based on eligibility; separate Medicare Part D needed if applicable.[3]
